title: Graph Neural Networks — Message Passing & Aggregation
summary: Neural networks that operate on graph-structured data (nodes + edges). Core idea: each node aggregates information from its neighbors (message passing), then updates its own representation. Repeated over L layers, each node's representation captures its L-hop neighborhood. Directly relevant to knowledge graph interest.
key_insights: Message passing: each node collects features from its neighbors, aggregates them (sum/mean/max), updates its own feature
L layers of message passing = each node 'sees' its L-hop neighborhood
Inductive bias: graph structure determines information flow (contrast: transformer = fully connected, CNN = grid)
GCN (Graph Convolutional Network): simplest form — normalized sum of neighbor features
GraphSAGE: learns to sample and aggregate from neighbors — scalable to large graphs
Connection to attention: Graph Attention Network (GAT) uses attention weights on edges — transformer attention on a graph

mathematical_core.gcn: H^(l+1) = σ(D̃^{-½} Ã D̃^{-½} H^(l) W^(l)) — normalized adjacency × features × weights
mathematical_core.gat: α_{vu} = softmax_u(LeakyReLU(aᵀ[Wh_v || Wh_u])) — attention on edges

related: TRANSFORMER.SELF_ATTENTION — self-attention is GNN on a fully-connected graph
TRANSFORMER.CROSS_ATTENTION — cross-attention between graph nodes and query = knowledge-grounded attention
GNN.GAT — attention mechanism applied to graph edges

notes: Learning plan Week 8 topic. Directly connects to knowledge graph + cross-attention idea discussed during assessment.
